# Transcrição: 01-The Curse of Knowledge

- **Fonte:** 01-The Curse of Knowledge
- **Duração:** 04:43
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Avoiding the curse of knowledge, the first way to avoid it is to identify your blind spots. These are the areas that you are likely to speak about that normal people will not understand. For my daughter, it is mythology. She comes down the stairs every day. She sees something and says, you know what that reminds me of? And then she speaks for 13 minutes or hours or days about some mythological character who I've never heard of before that I can make no connection to. She suffers from the curse of knowledge. She thinks I know something about Greek or Roman or Indian or Tahitian mythology. And I don't. And therefore her stories about mythology do not connect with me. If she wanted to better connect with me, she would identify mythology as one of her blind spots, an area that she has expertise that I do not, and she should probably acknowledge I don't care about it either. But for you, what you want to do is identify your areas of expertise. Identify the things that you understand deeply that normal human beings, the average human being, your customers, your clients, your partners, your investors, the people sitting in your audience, might not understand. That means if you're a tech firm, there's one million technical aspects of your business that most people will not understand. And if you're trying to sell your product and you're diving into the tech, you're going to have a problem. With attorneys, it's often the language that they use. They roll out words that they're so accustomed to because they use them every day in court, yet when they say them to a jury or they say them to one of their clients in their office or they're speaking to a potential witness, they lose those people completely. When I work with the clergy, when I'm working with a priest or a minister, they quote biblical scriptures as if I know what the biblical scripture is. They'll say something like, well, you know, in 4 Corinthians, they say kind of the same thing as if I know what 4 Corinthians is, but they're so deeply in touch with their religious documents and texts that they can't imagine someone who might not be. They've said to me things like, well, I mean, it's 4 Corinthians. Who doesn't know 4 Corinthians? And I want to say, well, I don't go to church and my children are Jewish, and so is my wife. We don't know 4 Corinthians. I don't know it at all, but they don't understand that. They have to identify their blind spots. So you're going to take a moment now and you're going to identify your blind spots. You're going to pick out the areas of expertise that you have and try to clear them out if possible. I'm aware of my blind spots. Earlier in this course, I used movies as references to help you understand storytelling. There are a million movies that I could use. I understand that some of the movies I used, you may not be aware of. I'm deeply aware of that. The only way that I can counteract that problem is by choosing extremely popular movies,

## [03:04 - 04:43]

which is what I do. Titanic is the number two movie of all time, box office. So it makes sense to me that I'll use that movie if I must. I choose movies like Star Wars and Raiders of the Lost Ark and Jurassic Park because these were blockbuster movies, but I know you may have not seen any of them. And if that's the case, I'm sorry. That is the curse of knowledge. That is me being aware of something that you are not aware of. Now, I am tied to the idea that I need to use movies as comparisons because, as you've heard me say many times, stories are movies we create in the mind. But the best I can do is choose the most popular movies and hope you've seen at least some of them. You need to do the same. Identify those blind spots, those things that you have enormous knowledge about, and then understand that rather than speaking about those things in their technical or complicated or specific terms, quite often a metaphor is required or additional explanation is required. Or another way to describe it comparing it to something else is required. You have to take those blind spots and either get rid of them or find ways to mitigate them. Find ways to describe the things you're trying to describe in ways that are more basic for human beings. Take a moment, write down those potential blind spots now. Understand what they are so that you can begin to eliminate them or mitigate them completely. Go ahead.
